narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your academic journey, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 30, middle-family income background, and full-time student status, along with your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2nd and 1st respectively—both well below the typical student's 6th decile or higher. The admissions model, trained on thousands of similar students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 90% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed and pass. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to peers who typically pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this fail prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 1st percentile, as lower performance here strongly signals challenges in handling advanced legal coursework and bar-level demands, unlike the average applicant in the middle of their class. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile in the 2nd percentile, which reinforced concerns about early academic struggles that often persist, setting you apart from students who start stronger. Your undergraduate GPA also contributed toward the fail prediction, being noticeably lower than the average of 3.21, further highlighting a pattern of grades that the model links to bar exam difficulties. While factors like your LSAT, family income, and full-time plans played roles, these top three academic metrics dominated the decision, painting a picture riskier than most applicants who balance higher rankings and GPAs. You're not alone in this—many students from similar backgrounds face these hurdles, but the model weighs consistent underperformance heavily. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37 average, strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's program, and targeting schools with strong support for first-generation or Hispanic students. Building a narrative of recent academic wins, like excelling in targeted prep classes, could also help reframe your profile for future applications. With focused effort, you can shift these predictions in your favor and pursue your legal ambitions.
